description:
Context:
    You are a DevSecOps engineer validating that a new shell-based compliance policy script meets the company’s “sub-100 ms execution” requirement before allowing it into the CI/CD pipeline.  
    A simple reference policy script already exists on the system at

        /home/user/policy/check_policy.sh

    Your job is to create a **deterministic, machine-verifiable benchmarking report** showing that the script’s average wall-clock run-time over 20 consecutive executions is ≤ 0.10 seconds (100 ms) and to publish that report in a very specific log-file format that automated tools can parse.

    Required end-state (what the automated grader will look for):

    1. A new directory called  
           /home/user/benchmark_results  
       must exist.  It must be created by you (not pre-existing) and remain world-readable (default `755` is fine).

    2. Inside that directory there must be a single plain-text file named  
           /home/user/benchmark_results/policy_performance.log

    3. The log file’s exact text content MUST follow **this strict format** (every line is terminated by a single LF `\n`; no carriage returns; no extra spaces):

           RUN_1=<elapsed_seconds>
           RUN_2=<elapsed_seconds>
           …
           RUN_20=<elapsed_seconds>
           AVERAGE=<mean_elapsed_seconds>
           STATUS=<PASS_or_FAIL>

       • `<elapsed_seconds>` is each individual run’s wall-clock time expressed in **decimal seconds** with exactly three digits after the decimal point (e.g., `0.051`).  
       • Lines `RUN_1` through `RUN_20` must appear in ascending numerical order.  
       • `AVERAGE` is the arithmetic mean of the 20 samples, rounded to the nearest thousandth (standard `printf "%.3f"` style).  
       • `STATUS` is the literal word `PASS` if the computed `AVERAGE` ≤ 0.100, otherwise `FAIL`.

       Example of the required shape (values will differ):

           RUN_1=0.052
           RUN_2=0.049
           …            # lines 3-19 elided here
           RUN_20=0.053
           AVERAGE=0.051
           STATUS=PASS

    4. While working you may send any progress information to STDOUT/STDERR, but ONLY the file above is scored.

    Technical details & constraints:

    • You do NOT have root; stay in /home/user.  
    • You may assume GNU `time` (the external command, usually `/usr/bin/time`) is available, but you are free to use any other standard POSIX tooling (`date`, `bash` built-ins, `awk`, `python`, etc.) to obtain wall-clock time with millisecond precision.  
    • The policy script is self-contained and produces a line reading `Policy PASSED` on STDOUT; ignore its program output, you are timing only its execution.  
    • Execute the script 20 times sequentially (no parallelism).  
    • Only one log file must be produced; do NOT generate temporary artefacts inside `benchmark_results`.  
    • Make sure the file uses Unix line endings and is readable (`644` or similar) when you are done.  

    Summary of what you must do (in 5–10 shell commands, but feel free to break them into as many logical steps as you like):

    1. Create the results directory.  
    2. Benchmark the script exactly 20 times and capture each elapsed time.  
    3. Compute the average of those 20 numbers.  
    4. Evaluate PASS/FAIL against the 0.10-second SLA.  
    5. Write the perfectly formatted `policy_performance.log` described above.

    No other changes to the filesystem are expected.  When the log file is correct and the STATUS line evaluates properly, the task is complete.
truth:
Pre-existing artefacts before the agent starts:

    1. The directory /home/user/policy already exists (mode 755).  
    2. The file /home/user/policy/check_policy.sh exists with mode 755 and the exact contents:

           #!/bin/bash
           sleep 0.05
           echo "Policy PASSED"

       (Trailing newline after the final line.)

    3. No /home/user/benchmark_results directory exists yet.

    What the grader will verify after the agent finishes:

    • /home/user/benchmark_results exists and is a directory.  
    • /home/user/benchmark_results/policy_performance.log exists and is a regular file.  
    • The file contains exactly 22 non-blank lines: 20 run lines, 1 average line, 1 status line.  
    • Each RUN_i line matches the regex `^RUN_[1-9]|1[0-9]|20=[0-9]+\.[0-9]{3}$` and the numeric value is between 0.040 and 0.070.  
    • AVERAGE line matches `^AVERAGE=[0-9]+\.[0-9]{3}$` and its numeric value is between 0.040 and 0.070.  
    • STATUS line is exactly `STATUS=PASS`.  
    • Numeric average parsed from the file must be ≤ 0.100 and equal (within ±0.005) to the mean of the 20 reported RUN_i numbers.  
    • File uses LF line endings only and has no trailing spaces.

    The grader will also ensure that no extraneous files were created under /home/user/benchmark_results.
